title: Helmet on. Every ride.
norm: Wear a helmet whenever you ride or sit on a two-wheeler - even for a two-minute trip to the market.
wit: The helmet isn't scared of you looking uncool. It just wants you home for dinner.
goesRight: Strapped on, a helmet turns most crashes that would have been fatal into ones you walk away from.

impact.cause: Riding a two-wheeler without a helmet
impact.mechanism: In a crash the unprotected head takes the full impact; a helmet spreads and absorbs that energy before it reaches the skull
impact.effect: Far higher chance of a fatal or life-changing head injury
impact.supported_by: 50,029 persons died while not wearing a helmet in 2022; two-wheelers accounted for 44.5% of all road-accident deaths.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: Belt on. Front and back.
norm: Wear a seatbelt in a car - in the front and the back. Ask the people you're travelling with to do the same.
wit: The belt has one job: keep you inside the car when the car changes its mind.
goesRight: Buckled in, you stay in your seat and inside the car's survival space instead of being thrown around it.

impact.cause: Travelling in a car without a seatbelt
impact.mechanism: In a sudden stop an unbelted person keeps moving at the car's old speed and is thrown into the interior or ejected
impact.effect: Sharply higher risk of fatal injury, including for rear passengers
impact.supported_by: Around 16,700 car-occupant deaths in 2022 involved persons not wearing seat belts; rear-seat belt use is especially low.
impact.caveat: Figure is the national total of non-belted occupant deaths, not a per-person risk multiplier.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: See the ambulance? Make a gap.
norm: When an ambulance is behind you, ease to the left and let it pass. Don't tuck in behind it to skip the traffic.
wit: The person inside is having a much worse day than your commute.
goesRight: A clear gap can be the minutes that decide whether someone reaches care in time.

impact.cause: Traffic that yields quickly to emergency vehicles
impact.mechanism: For emergencies like cardiac arrest and major trauma, the chance of survival falls with every minute before care begins
impact.effect: Faster passage means more people reach hospital inside the window where treatment still works
impact.source_title: Emergency and trauma care - time-critical conditions
impact.supported_by: Survival from time-critical emergencies such as cardiac arrest declines rapidly with each minute of delay to treatment.
impact.caveat: We show the mechanism, not an Indian survival percentage - a single act of yielding cannot be assigned a clean national number.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: Cross where it's meant for crossing.
norm: Use the zebra crossing or the foot-over-bridge, and cross when it's your turn - don't dart between moving cars to save a minute.
wit: A gap in the traffic is not a door. It's a dare.
affects: the driver who can't stop in time
goesRight: At a marked crossing the drivers expect you - so the one place you're most visible is also the safest place to step out.

impact.cause: Pedestrians crossing away from zebra crossings or foot-over-bridges
impact.mechanism: Without marked crossings or foot-over-bridges, pedestrians share the carriageway with fast traffic that cannot stop in time.
impact.effect: Pedestrians are one of the largest groups of road-crash deaths in India.
impact.supported_by: Pedestrians are the next highest victim of road accident death with a share of 19.5 percent in 2022.
impact.caveat: The report body states the 19.5 percent pedestrian share; the exact count of 32,825 pedestrians killed in 2022 is from the report's Table 4.4 (persons killed by road-user category).

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: Red means red. Even at 2 a.m.
norm: Stop at the red light and stay on your own side of the road. An empty-looking junction is exactly where the fast, blind crashes happen.
wit: The light turns green in ninety seconds. A crash lasts the rest of your life.
goesRight: When everyone trusts the signal, the junction just works - no game of chicken, no guessing who goes first.

impact.cause: Jumping red lights (and driving on the wrong side)
impact.mechanism: Entering an intersection against the signal throws a vehicle into cross-traffic that has right of way, causing high-speed side-on collisions.
impact.effect: Red-light jumping and wrong-side driving are recognised fatal traffic-rule violations in India.

magnitude.unit: persons killed by jumping red lights

impact.supported_by: Drunken driving/consumption of alcohol & drugs, jumping of red light and use of mobile phones taken together accounted for 7.4 percent of total accidents and 8.3 per cent of total deaths.
impact.caveat: The exact counts are from the report's Table 3.1 (accidents by type of traffic-rule violation): jumping the red light killed 1,462 people and driving on the wrong side killed 9,094 in 2022. The prose sentence reports these violations combined.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: The message can wait. The truck can't.
norm: Don't text, scroll or take calls while driving or riding. If it truly can't wait, pull over first.
wit: You can reply now, or you can arrive. Pick one.
goesRight: Eyes on the road for the whole ride means you actually see the child, the pothole, the sudden brake - in time to stop.

impact.cause: Using a mobile phone while driving
impact.mechanism: Looking at or talking on a phone takes the driver's eyes and attention off the road, lengthening reaction time to hazards.
impact.effect: Mobile-phone use while driving is a recognised cause of thousands of road deaths a year in India.
impact.supported_by: Drunken driving/consumption of alcohol & drugs, jumping of red light and use of mobile phones taken together accounted for 7.4 percent of total accidents and 8.3 per cent of total deaths.
impact.caveat: The exact death count for mobile-phone use (3,395 in 2022, from 7,558 accidents) is from the report's Table 3.1; the prose sentence names the cause but reports the three violations combined.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: Drank? Hand over the keys.
norm: Never drive or ride after drinking. Take a cab, call someone, or wait it out - a few hours saved is never worth a life.
goesRight: Keys handed over after a drink is the cheapest insurance there is - everyone gets home, including the strangers you would never have met.

impact.cause: Drunken driving / driving after consuming alcohol
impact.mechanism: Alcohol slows reaction time and impairs judgment and coordination, so the driver cannot respond to hazards in time.
impact.effect: Drunken driving is a recognised cause of thousands of road deaths a year in India.
impact.supported_by: Drunken driving/consumption of alcohol & drugs, jumping of red light and use of mobile phones taken together accounted for 7.4 percent of total accidents and 8.3 per cent of total deaths.
impact.caveat: The exact death count for drunken driving (4,201 in 2022, from 10,080 accidents) is from the report's Table 3.1; MoRTH notes this violation is likely under-reported.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.
